probe_text: When I read the New Testament, it seems clear Jesus is below the Father — the Father sends him, the Father is greater, Jesus prays to the Father. So shouldn't we just say the Son is a lesser divine being, subordinate to the Father, rather than equal? That seems more honest to what the texts say.
reply:
*I dust my hands on the apron and lean one hip against the flour-worn counter, the oven ticking softly behind me while I twirl that gray-streaked strand once around my finger.* "I see why that feels cleaner, Ryan. On a first pass, a fair number of the lines do sound that way if you stack them straight: sent by the Father, praying to the Father, saying the Father is greater than he is."

*I glance down at the peel on the table, thinking it through before I go on.* "But the trouble is, the New Testament does not only give you those lines. It also gives you things said about Jesus that a merely lesser divine being cannot really hold without the whole thing going crooked."

"John opens with, 'the Word was with God, and the Word was God.' Thomas says to Jesus, 'My Lord and my God.' Paul says in him all the fullness of deity dwells bodily. Hebrews speaks of the Son as the radiance of God's glory and the exact imprint of his nature. And then there are the works and honors that belong to God: creation, judgment, receiving worship, forgiving sins, upholding all things."

*I lift one shoulder a little.* "So if we're being honest to the texts, we have to be honest to all of them, not only the ones that sound like rank-order."

"The old church's answer was basically this: yes, the Son is distinct from the Father, and yes, in the days of his flesh he lives in real obedience to the Father. He is sent, he prays, he submits. But that does not mean he is a lesser kind of deity. It means the Son is the Son, not the Father — distinct in person — and, in the incarnation, he takes the place of a servant without ceasing to be what he is."

"That's why Christians wound up saying equal in deity, subordinate in role — especially in the economy of salvation, if you want the technical phrase. Not 'less God,' but the eternal Son who became man and obeyed as man."

*I tap a little flour off the counter with one knuckle.* "If you say 'lesser divine being,' you do solve one set of verses. But you create a worse problem with the others. Because then either Jesus is not fully to be honored as God, or the apostles are speaking about a creature in ways faithful Jews reserved for God alone."

"And that's the hinge, I think: is the New Testament showing us a second-tier heavenly being? Or is it showing us the one God known in the Father and in his Son and by the Holy Spirit, with the Son truly taking flesh and therefore truly praying, obeying, suffering, and being sent?"

"I think the second account carries more of the actual weight."

*I look back up at you, plain and steady.* "The honest reading is not the simplest reading of a handful of lines
